SECTION 2 Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
Introduction and Preferred Terminology
T﻿he loss or absence of a limb is a unique and profound condition 
in which a person has lost a very tangible part of oneself. Many 
patients and even health care providers have an understandable 
anxiety about the future following amputation. In fact, when 
patients with diabetes were asked about what complications they 
fear most, they rated amputation as more frightening than 
death.45 With today’s rehabilitation and prosthetic technology, 
this sobering outlook need not be the case, especially for indi -
viduals with upper extremity limb loss, who in many cases have a 
good prognosis for functional restoration and adaptation. In 
many cases, amputation can provide the opportunity for better 
function. Even patients with bilateral amputation or significant 
comorbidities can lead fulfilling and active lives. Despite the high 
visibility of the condition, amputees are frequently underserved. 
Even within health care systems with well-developed amputee 
rehabilitation programs, patients with limb loss may never receive 
specialized amputation rehabilitation care or even receive pros -
thetic training. 36 We hope that this chapter aids our readers in 
meeting this unmet need by improving care within your sphere of 
practice for patients with limb loss and limb difference.
T﻿his chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the evalua-
tion and management of upper limb loss and limb difference pa -
tients, with a focus on relevant information to physiatrists and 
other rehabilitation professionals. We will discuss background 
information regarding both adult and pediatric amputation and 
limb differences, surgical considerations and practices, prosthetic 
evaluation and prescription, as well as the management of comor-
bidities and rehabilitation complications as the patient progresses 
from the surgical stage to acute rehabilitation and then to long-
term follow-up. We will discuss the impact of emerging technolo-
gies on the field and important considerations for reintegration 
and adaptation to limb loss.
T erminology in clinical practice, such as amputation and limb 
loss, are used interchangeably in this chapter, as well as the equiva-
lent terms amputee and person with limb loss. T﻿he term residual limb 
or residuum is generally preferred as the medical term for the post-
amputation limb, replacing older common terms such as stump. 
T﻿he sound limb conventionally refers to the nonamputated side. 
T﻿he term limb difference denotes a patient with a nonstandard limb 
who did not undergo an amputation, but instead has a congeni -
tally absent or malformed limb. T erminology preference is patient 
and context specific and evolves over time. What matters most is 
that health care providers and patients understand one another.
Limb Loss Classification
T﻿he anatomy of various amputation locations is an important first 
step in understanding their functional impact and rehabilitation 
• Prosthetic training phases: Prosthetic training is divided into 
preprosthetic, early prosthetic, and advanced prosthetic phases; 
each is aimed at maximizing functional adaptation.
• Pain management: Residual limb pain and phantom limb pain 
must be addressed during rehabilitation to prevent prosthetic 
abandonment.
• Financial considerations: The cost of prosthetics can range from 
$3,000 to over $40,000, and insurance coverage varies. Patients 
should be guided through the financial realities of prosthetic 
care.
• Special considerations: Bilateral amputees, children, and patients 
with additional impairments such as traumatic brain injury may 
require specialized prosthetic devices and training approaches.
10
Rehabilitation and Prosthetic 
Restoration in Upper Limb Amputation
JOHN R. HERMANSEN AND NATHAN T. MCKENTY
• Prognosis and adaptation: Upper extremity amputees generally 
have a good prognosis for functional restoration, especially 
with modern rehabilitation and prosthetic technology.
• Terminology: Preferred terms include residual limb rather than 
stump and limb loss or limb difference  over amputation. 
Terminology should be patient specific.
• Classification of amputation: Upper limb amputations are 
 classified into digit amputations (most common), minor 
 amputations (below the wrist), and major amputations (at or 
above the wrist).
• Prosthetic options: Four categories of upper limb prostheses 
 exist: passive, body powered, myoelectric, and hybrid. Each is 
tailored to the patient’s functional and vocational goals.
SUMMARY PEARLS

SECTION 2  Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
strategies. T﻿h e most common types of upper extremity amputa -
tions are depicted in Fig. 10.1. Digit amputations are the most 
common (78%) of upper extremity amputations. 10 By conven-
tion, a minor limb amputation is one occurring below the wrist, 
while those occurring at or above the wrist are termed major limb 
amputation. A digit amputation’s functional effects depend on the 
digit involved and the activities of the patient. T﻿he thumb is 
critical for palmar grip, adduction pinch, and oppositional pinch, 
but it may be replaced with a thumb driver or similar prosthesis 
to restore function. T﻿he index finger interacts intimately with the 
thumb for many of these functions, but if lost or greatly short -
ened, this function can often be subsumed by the third digit. 
Amputation involving the third and fourth digits is less function-
ally impactful and is often restored with a primarily cosmetic 
prosthesis. T﻿he fifth digit’s importance and strength in hook grasp 
is often underappreciated, especially for patients participating in 
manual labor. From a surgical perspective, retaining a greater 
length of any of these digits will improve function. In some cases, 
however, a hand may be more functional with a more extensive 
ray amputation than a very short residual finger.
30 Surgical recon-
structive techniques, such as phalangization or pollicization, can 
rearrange and modify remaining digits to maximize function in 
some patients. Wrist disarticulations provide a greater degree of 
supination and pronation than more proximal transradial ampu-
tations, but they present prosthetic difficulties due to their long 
length, making prosthesis use difficult and arm length asymmetric 
to the sound limb.

T ransradial amputations are the most common type of major 
upper limb amputation and are subcategorized by their residual 
bone length from the end of the limb to the medial epicondyle. 
Generally, a longer limb retains more supination/pronation, 
about 50% of which can be transferred to a prosthesis. A longer 
limb also provides a longer lever arm for elbow flexion strength.
Elbow disarticulations are less common and are most fre-
quently seen in pediatric patients to preserve the bone’s terminal 
growth plate. While they can be accommodated by modern tech-
niques for cosmesis, the residual limb is difficult to fit with a 
natural and functional prosthetic elbow joint due to its length and 
may result in arm asymmetry.
T ranshumeral amputations are also classified by their length, 
with a longer limb preserving more glenohumeral motion and 
strength. As more of the limb’s length is reduced, the prosthetic 
socket must encompass more of the shoulder and inhibit range of 
motion. At levels of amputation higher than the humeral neck, 
shoulder range of motion is largely lost. T﻿his includes glenohu -
meral disarticulation and more proximal forequarter amputation, 
which are normally performed for local malignancy or severe 
trauma. While these amputations present difficulties in fitting a 
prosthesis, newer myoelectric technology is broadening the func -
tional possibilities for these patients, as we will discuss later in the 
chapter.
Demographics, Incidence, and Prevalence  
of Adult Limb Loss
About 185,000 persons undergo an amputation in the United 
States yearly. In 2008, the prevalence of all limb loss nationwide 
was estimated at 1.9 million people, of which about 500,000 are 
living with a minor upper limb amputation and 41,000 with a 
major upper limb amputation.
4 While the number of people liv -
ing with lower extremity limb loss in the United States is expected 
to double by the year 2050 (largely due to rates of dysvascular 
disease, obesity, and diabetes), the etiologies of upper extremity 
limb loss differ significantly, and their incidence is generally ex -
pected to remain stable or slightly decrease over the coming years, 
partly thanks to improved workplace safety standards and ad -
vancements in surgical limb salvage, limb replantation, and even 
limb transplantation. Of upper limb amputations, 80% are due 
to trauma, with most occurring at work involving saws, blades, or 
other machinery in the civilian population. Active military per -
sonnel experience a higher rate of amputations due to blast or 
gunshot injuries and have a higher incidence of concomitant 
traumatic brain injury, multiple limb amputation, visual and 
hearing loss, soft tissue loss, and burns.
9
Other causes of upper limb loss include burns, peripheral vas-
cular disease and clot-related ischemia, infection, and malignancy. 
T﻿here are also rare cases of planned, elective amputation in unusual 
scenarios such as severe neurologic injury causing limb paralysis, 
severe nonfunctional contracture, and congenital deformity. In 
general, males account for more than 75% of upper limb loss, and 
the more severe the injury the more likely the victim is male.
2
The Rehabilitation Team and the Role of 
Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation
As with other types of rehabilitation patients, those with limb loss 
are most successful when cared for by a well-coordinated interdis-
ciplinary team of professionals (Fig. 10.2
). T﻿he surgical team var-
ies depending on the etiology of amputation, but may include

trauma, orthopedics, plastic, and vascular surgeons. T﻿he primary 
care physician is an indispensable part of the team for managing 
general medical care and comorbidities and helping to coordinate 
referrals. T﻿he physical and occupational therapists ideally have 
experience working with upper extremity amputees, with or with-
out a prosthetic device. T﻿hese professionals help ensure a patient 
is ready to manage the activities of daily life and help to recom -
mend assistive equipment. Psychiatry and psychology profession-
als can offer counseling and support services for the patient and 
families and can help process the emotional journey of limb loss 
and limb difference, and help to connect the patient to commu -
nity and vocational resources.
T﻿he prosthetist is a particularly important part of the team. 
T﻿he prosthetist evaluates the patient and then recommends, fab -
ricates, and fits the prosthesis for the patient. Going forward, the 
prosthetist offers important follow-up care for adjustments and 
troubleshooting of prosthetic issues and acts as a resource to the 
health care provider and patient to help navigate the expanding 
variety of prosthetic componentry available today.
Other team members in the community include patient advo-
cates, social workers and case managers, and certified peer visitors. 
When available, a professional patient advocate can be helpful to 
a patient in navigating the health care system, particularly around 
issues of insurance coverage and appointment access. Similarly, a 
social worker or case manager can assist in several ways, such as 
coordinating insurance communication, preparing follow-up ap -
pointments, and ordering equipment after discharge. In cases 
where workers’ compensation is involved, this case manager may 
also help coordinate coverage and compensation for the patient.
A certified peer visitor (CPV) is an individual outside of the 
patient’s family or care team that has usually had a similar experi-
ence and can provide support and guidance but not medical ad-
vice. T﻿he CPV may have similar limb loss or limb difference and 
may have experience as a caregiver. Community organizations,

such as the Amputee Coalition of America (ACA), can offer ad -
ditional resources and connect to local support groups with com-
munity and recreational opportunities. T﻿h e ACA also administers 
the CPV training program, and a CPV can be requested by the 
patient or care team through the website. Family members and 
other caregivers are indispensable parts of the patient’s recovery 
and reintegration, too.
While some of these professionals may only be part of the 
patient’s team for a short time, the physiatrist and other rehabili-
tation professionals may be lifelong partners. Physical medicine 
and rehabilitation is uniquely positioned to help lead and coordi-
nate this team, and it is their responsibility to best align the pa -
tient’s medical recovery to the context of a broader life. Where 
possible, a physiatrist should begin evaluating and educating the 
patient prior to surgery, allowing the patient to set goals and ex -
pectations for recovery. In some cases, the rehabilitation team may 
consult with and advise the surgical team on ways to optimize 
postoperative function and prosthetic fit, considering the patient’s 
personal, professional, and recreational activities, prior level of 
function, and comorbidities. After surgery, the physiatrist’s long-
term goals are to maximize performance and decrease pain. Por -
tions of the ongoing care will include referring for therapy and 
other services, evaluating and prescribing prosthetic devices or 
other equipment, managing pain, and preventing and monitoring 
complications. Amputees will experience several stages of care, 
including surgery, postoperative care and acute rehabilitation, and 
advanced stages of rehabilitation. We discuss each stage of this 
process in the next sections of the chapter.
Surgical Considerations
Presurgical Evaluation
When evaluating the patient prior to surgery, the physiatrist 
should introduce the concept of the interdisciplinary team (see 
earlier). Education about amputation should be given, and expec-
tations and goals should be established for the timeline of recov -
ery. Almost all patients will not leave the hospital with a prosthe -
sis, and most patients should plan for this process to take weeks 
to months (see later). Explain to the patient what to expect from 
surgery, acute rehabilitation, and introduce the concept of the 
rehabilitation team, with different team members available to help 
the patient succeed after limb loss. At this stage, collecting a rel -
evant physiatric history will help to anticipate the patient’s likely 
needs upon discharge and determine candidacy for an eventual 
prosthesis. We recommend delaying in-depth discussion of pros -
thetic candidacy until after surgery for most patients, by which 
time acute medical circumstances have stabilized and patients 
have been evaluated by occupational and physical therapists.
Rehabilitation and Surgical Planning
Rehabilitation physicians should be aware of the surgical objec -
tives and techniques frequently used in this population. T﻿he 
general goals of upper extremity amputation surgery 32 are shown 
in Box 10.1.
Whether to pursue limb salvage or amputation, and at what 
length to amputate, depends on many variables and remains a 
subject of debate. It is difficult to predict individual patient out-
comes, and there is no firmly agreed upon formula for recom -
mending a level or time of amputation for every patient. Most 
physicians would ascribe to preserving as much limb as possible.

SECTION 2  Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
A long transradial or long transhumeral amputation (preserving 
.60% length of the respective bones) generally provides better 
function and prosthetic use than a more proximal amputation 
through the same bone. Because the patient may already have 
traumatic or vascular compromise of tissue, the functionally ideal 
length is not always surgically feasible. In determining the level of 
the amputation, physicians consider the condition of nearby soft 
tissues, bone defects, proximal joint function, vascular status, and 
injuries to the contralateral side. If remaining soft tissue is insuf -
ficient to cover the residual limb or would be under excessive 
tension, a higher level of amputation may have improved out-
comes. When there is injury to the bilateral limbs, every effort 
should be made to salvage the less injured side for a better func -
tional outcome, even if it requires excessive shortening of the re -
sidual limb. Attempting limb salvage may also give the patient 
more time to make informed choices about further amputation 
procedures after the acute trauma and usually does not preclude 
the patient from undergoing a later amputation. For these rea -
sons, limb salvage may be pursued even if complete success is 
unlikely.
A longer residual limb usually lends greater strength and range 
of motion, but it may not be ideal for every patient. For example, 
a wrist or elbow disarticulation preserves more length than a more 
proximal surgery, but a new prosthetic joint cannot be located at 
the same position as the original anatomic joint. T﻿his creates 
asymmetric limb length, poor prosthetic cosmesis, and, in some 
cases, reduced function and limited prosthetic componentry 
choices. Despite these drawbacks, an elbow disarticulation does 
usually provide better control of a prosthetic and better muscle 
attachment than a transhumeral amputation. While some pa -
tients may prefer this, others may prefer a more inconspicuous 
transhumeral prosthesis. Open and frank discussions are para -
mount in this decision. In dysvascular or infected patients, risks 
of poor healing or further tissue loss must also be considered 
against the potential benefits of attempting to preserve a joint. 
Patient preferences and lifestyle goals should always be consid -
ered, and it is often the role of the physiatrist and other rehabilita-
tion team members to bring these factors into the surgical discus-
sion. As always, the best decisions are made by a fully informed 
patient under the advisement of the interdisciplinary team.
T﻿here are surgical alternatives to amputation in select patients, 
such as the replantation of a severed limb. While it is a rare pro -
cedure, limb transplantation is also an area of active research. 
Upper limb amputees with blindness, such as those injured by a 
blast trauma, may have very limited ability to functionally use a 
terminal device because of the need for visual feedback. T﻿h is rare 
situation could be an indication for a Krukenberg procedure, in 
which the radius and ulna are divided, and muscle groups rear -
ranged to form a pincerlike residual limb that retains tactile

also historically been performed in resource-poor settings even

can also be fit with cosmetic prosthetics for social settings. 
 Reconstructive surgeries may be performed after amputation to 
 optimize patient function. T﻿hese include index pollicization, 
metacarpal lengthening, phalangization, and even toe-to-thumb 
transfer.  Although fur ther details of these procedures are beyond 
the scope of this chapter, rehabilitation physicians should be 
aware of these options to aid in advising the patient and the 
surgical team.
Surgical Techniques: Soft Tissue Management
T﻿hree primary techniques utilized in amputation surgery for clo -
sure of the residual limb are myofascial closure, myoplasty, and 
myodesis. Myofascial closure involves approximating the outer 
fascial envelope over the muscles to close the limb. T﻿his approxi-
mates only loose tissues and does so under minimal tension to the 
underlying muscles. T﻿his is commonly thought to provide some 
benefit to healing in dysvascular patients, but this theory has lim-
ited empiric evidence. Myoplasty is the connection of opposing 
muscle groups to cover the transected bone end. T﻿his provides 
greater stability than myofascial closure but can cause pain with 
movement of these muscles as well as potential functional and 
prosthetic difficulties. Myodesis is the attachment of the residual 
limb’s muscle groups to the end of the bone, either through drill

holes or through connection to the periosteum. T﻿his technique 
takes the most surgical time but prevents motion over the bone 
end and provides padding between the bone and a new prosthetic 
socket. In transradial amputations, for example, multiple muscle 
groups are available to perform myodesis to bone. T﻿his technique 
maximizes the distal soft tissue padding and control of the resid -
ual limb. While most amputations require at least partial transec-
tion of the muscle belly, in some cases a tendon may be available 
to attach to bone instead. T﻿his tenodesis provides the highest qual-
ity of muscle stabilization due to the good purchase of suture into 
bone with the tendon tissue.32
Surgical management of skin tension is of paramount impor -
tance for healing and for functional use of a residual limb. T﻿he 
end of the residual limb must be adequately covered and padded 
with tissue. Efforts are made to avoid the formation of adherent, 
thin, and scarred skin covering, which is prone to injury and 
slow healing. In some traumatic and infected limbs, there may 
be multistage surgeries, where a first closure is allowed to heal 
partially and then later finalized. T﻿his may include skin traction, 
in which the wound is partially closed over a vacuum-assisted 
closure device (commonly called a wound vac) and allowed to 
begin healing. By maintaining tension over the end of the bone, 
muscle and cutaneous flaps are prevented from retracting as the 
limb heals. Infected wounds may require multiple debridement 
procedures to fully excise necrotic tissue and preserve as much 
healthy tissue as possible. Later, primary closure is performed 
with typical myodesis and tenodesis techniques. Skin grafting 
may be used to repair defects or provide viable tissue to com -
plete a covering. Other concomitant injuries may complicate 
surgical planning and functionality, such as joint contracture or 
extensive burns.
Surgical Techniques: Nerve Management
Neuromas are a common complication following amputation. 
Neuromas are disorganized overgrowths of nerve tissue that form 
in the majority of amputees after a nerve transection. T﻿hese neu-
romas may or may not be symptomatic, and who will develop 
symptomatic neuromas is currently impossible to predict. Symp -
tomatic neuromas can result in both residual limb pain and phan-
tom limb pain.
Nerve management in amputation surgery is a primary objec -
tive for long-term success. T﻿he traditional nerve management 
technique is a traction neurectomy, in which nerves in the ampu-
tated limb are placed under moderate tension and then tran -
sected. T﻿he nerves are then allowed to retract proximally into the 
wound and muscles of the residual limb. T﻿his technique accepts 
that a neuroma is almost certain to form but moves it away from 
high-pressure areas where it is more likely to become irritated and 
symptomatic. Other techniques place the cut ends of nerves into 
muscle tissue or bone near the end of the residual limb. In some 
patients, however, symptomatic neuromas still develop. T﻿he latter 
technique results in neuromas close to the distal end of the re -
sidual limb and can predispose to socket pressure and irritation in 
a prosthesis.
Recent advances in surgical technique are changing many 
practices and may improve outcomes. T wo conceptually related 
procedures are targeted muscle reinnervation (TMR) and the re -
generative peripheral nerve interface (RPNI). T﻿hese procedures 
were originally developed to give patients more control over myo-
electric prostheses but were also found to improve pain outcomes 
in amputees and reduce the incidence of neuroma formation. 11 
Surgical details are beyond the scope of this chapter, but both 
techniques involve rewiring the limb’s transected nerves into 
 remaining neuromuscular targets, thereby reducing the chance of 
neuroma formation and pain. T﻿hey can be performed at the time 
of amputation or later as a secondary surgery. Availability of these 
procedures is highly surgeon dependent, but based on patient 
outcomes, these techniques are likely to become more common. 
TMR is a procedure in which the transected ends of nerve from 
an amputation are anastomosed to motor entry points in remain-
ing muscles. T﻿his differs from previous techniques by supplying 
the transected nerve with viable perineurium and an active muscle 
to innervate. T﻿his may reduce the development of symptomatic 
neuromas and pain.6,25
TMR can be performed primarily at the time of surgery or 
secondarily at a later time as a treatment for symptomatic neuro -
mas or phantom limb pain. RPNI is a newer technique in which 
transected nerves are implanted into small free muscle flaps, 
which are harvested from elsewhere. T﻿his provides new localized 
myoelectric signals for myoelectric prosthesis control. Based on 
available data, RPNI appears to have a similar effect to TMR on 
reducing neuroma incidence and phantom pain.22 T﻿he procedures 
were originally designed to improve myoelectric prosthesis control 
by providing additional myosignals. T﻿his can be especially useful 
for patients with proximal upper extremity amputations who 
need to control complex prosthetics with a limited number of 
remaining muscles.
Another recent innovation is the agonist-antagonist myoneural 
interface (AMI) procedure. Although the details are beyond the 
scope of this chapter, AMI can provide patients with improved 
prosthetic control and bidirectional signaling between their limb 
and a prosthetic device.23 Human trials have shown potential for 
this to improve key skills, such as prosthetic control, sensory feed-
back, and proprioception. 41 T﻿hese and other promising proce -
dures may enhance the abilities of patients with limb loss and 
prosthetics.
Surgical Techniques: Osseointegration
Osseointegration (OI) is a recent advancement in amputation 
care. It involves the implantation of a prosthetic interface directly 
into the bone of a residual limb, with the prosthetic attachment 
point penetrating through the skin. Once healed, this implant 
provides the direct connection of the patient’s skeleton to a pros-
thetic limb. T﻿hese patients do not use a socket and as such may 
experience improved sensory feedback known as osseopercep -
tion.16 OI is most useful in a patient who cannot tolerate socket 
wear or when a short residual limb makes it overly difficult to fit 
a prosthetic socket. Implantation of the device commonly re -
quires two surgeries as well as extensive rehabilitation and time 
for healing before use. OI has been more extensively studied in 
the lower extremities, and guidelines for the upper extremity are 
less clear. T﻿his procedure is not currently US Food and Drug 
Administration (FDA) approved for the upper extremity. In the 
lower limbs, OI also comes with significant lifetime limitations 
on the use of the limb, such as the inability to jog, run, or carry 
heavy loads (cycling is still permitted). Risks of infection or im -
plant-related fracture are notable concerns but are often prevent -
able or treatable. Upper extremity patients may still seek out 
surgical specialists for this procedure abroad or through research 
programs within the United States. Anecdotal outcomes so far 
have been favorable, and it is likely that this will become increas -
ingly common.8

SECTION 2  Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
Elective Amputation
Elective amputation may be pursued in rare cases where an intact 
limb is severely limiting function or quality of life. Limbs that are 
severely painful, severely contracted, paralyzed, or otherwise non-
functional may prompt patients to seek out this option. Even if 
the patient may functionally benefit, it is important to ascertain if 
the patient’s expectations of prosthetic restoration are realistic. 
T﻿his is especially true in the upper extremity, where replacing the 
vast capabilities of a hand is not yet possible even with the most 
advanced prosthetics. Patients frequently overestimate the capa -
bilities of upper extremity prosthetic devices and underestimate 
their drawbacks. T﻿hey may have an unrealistic idea of which 
prosthetics they will use and have access to. Surgery itself carries 
inherent risk, and finding a surgeon willing to sacrifice an other -
wise viable limb may be challenging. If the patient is motivated 
by chronic pain, amputation is not a guarantee of pain control 
and may in some cases worsen the symptoms. Psychological 
evaluation is critical in cases where patients are considering such 
significant elective surgery. T﻿hese cases are patient specific, and 
the risks and benefits need to be deeply understood by all team 
members.38
Residual Limb Revision
Revision surgery can be performed in patients with prior amputa-
tion to address several issues. A surgical consultation may be indi-
cated if a patient is experiencing pain or skin breakdown that has 
not responded to conservative therapies or prosthetic modifica -
tions. Additionally, the residual limb may be a suboptimal shape 
for prosthetic fitting, and revision might improve function. Bony 
prominences or heterotopic bone growth can be shaven or beveled 
to reduce their effect on the limb. Excess scarring or soft tissue can 
be revised to improve prosthetic fit. As described previously, neu-
roma pain can be addressed through a variety of techniques. 
Other specialized procedures may be able to improve function in 
select patients, such as the Marquardt humeral angulation oste -
otomy, which creates a bony angulation at the terminal end of the 
residual humerus to create what is best described as an artificial 
humeral condyle. T﻿his allows for better prosthetic suspension and 
more rotational control of the residual limb than in a typical 
transhumeral amputation.
Perioperative and Postoperative 
Management of Amputation
Once the surgical amputation is completed, the rehabilitation 
team stays in close contact with the patient and family if present. 
T﻿his initial phase of recovery can be quite variable, depending on 
medical stability and tissue health during surgery. T﻿hese factors 
contribute to the final decisions for amputation length, soft tissue 
management, and skin and flap closure. Patients may also receive 
an initial guillotine amputation, which is a straight cut through the 
limb, to immediately address the necrotic and infected distal tis -
sue. T﻿his is temporarily dressed, and after a period of recovery and 
stabilization, the patient returns to the operating room so that the 
amputation can be formally completed and closed. As discussed 
earlier, many patients undergoing amputation are doing so on an 
urgent or emergent basis. Due to the uncertain nature and comor-
bidities present at the time of surgery, it is not uncommon for 
patients to require a short- to medium-length intensive care unit 
admission for medical stabilization. A consulting rehabilitation 
team can be quite helpful in this setting, in assisting with splinting 
and bracing, therapy guidance, pain management, and early equip-
ment recommendations. In general, the focus of the immediate 
postamputation period is to control pain and edema, promote 
wound healing, prevent contractures, initiate remobilization, assist 
with choosing a rehabilitation pathway, and continue the support-
ive counseling and education (Box 10.2). T﻿his plan must be indi-
vidualized to meet the needs of each patient.
Postoperative pain control often requires an early, aggressive 
approach that considers the multiple potential pain generators in 
the postsurgical period. Pain control is discussed in more detail in 
the subsequent chapter on lower extremity amputation.
Edema control begins once the last suture or staple is placed 
by the surgeon. If there is no contraindication and the surgeon 
has the appropriate training, an immediate postoperative rigid 
dressing can be placed in the operating room. T﻿his is a special 
cast placed on the residual limb by the surgeon or another certi -
fied professional. Using the rigid dressing immediately for con -
trol of edema leads to earlier wound healing and improved pain 
control due to a reduction of pain mediators in the accumulated 
interstitial fluid. Depending on the amputation level and length 
of rigid dressings, they may also help prevent contractures of the 
nearby joint. 5 Drawbacks of rigid dressings include a decreased 
ability to observe the wound, interference with drains and lines, 
and, if applied incorrectly, a risk of pressure injuries of the 
 residual limb. T﻿hese issues, combined with the experience re -
quired to place the dressings, means that many institutions do 
not regularly use them. T﻿his can be an excellent opportunity for 
the rehabilitation team to work with the surgeons to help im -
prove surgical outcomes. 43
If rigid dressings are not an option, then soft dressings that 
assist with mild compression may be used. Garments or sleeves 
with a moderate-high compression pressure, such as prosthetic 
liners and shrinkers, should be avoided at this time. If a compres-
sive dressing or wrap is used, it is important to be evenly distrib -
uted along the limb length to avoid any focal areas of pressure or 
strangulation points immediately distal to joints.
Passive ranging of the joints in the limb should be done daily 
during recovery to maintain mobility. If joints are noted to be 
losing range in the postoperative period, then increasing the fre -
quency of ranging and/or utilizing appropriate bracing and splint-
ing should be considered. If the patient has preexisting increased 
muscle tone or contractures, then more specialized care may be 
indicated to help prevent worsening of these issues.
Assistance with recommending a rehabilitation pathway 
should also be an ongoing conversation with the patient and pri -
mary team. A hospital-based team of therapists, including physi -
cal and occupational therapists, should see the patient as soon as 
possible after surgery for initial assessments and goal setting.

and resources should all be considered. Patients with an existing 
contralateral hemiplegia from pathologies such as stroke or spinal 
cord injury, or similar functional deficits of the remaining upper

Patients who lose their dominant arm, especially if living alone 
prior to the amputation, may also require significant retraining to 
return home safely. In most cases, patients will be able to dis -

with the surgical and rehabilitation team on an outpatient basis. 
Early outpatient therapy will progress with an emphasis on recog-
nizing the abnormal postures and positioning that occur with 
basic activities of daily living (ADLs).
ADLs are retrained with one hand, and patients use adaptive 
equipment when appropriate. T﻿he amputee progresses from inde-
pendence with basic hygiene to advanced homemaking tasks. 
Hand dominance is retrained, when necessary, especially with 
handwriting and typing. Repetitive tasks can be used for strength-
ening. T﻿hese tasks include fine motor exercises and gross motor 
exercises with equipment and mirrors. Isometric exercises are ef -
fective in creating muscle bulk for stabilization of the arm in the 
socket of the prosthesis. Because balance is often disrupted in a 
new amputee, the goals should include strengthening of the 
trunk, core, and lower limbs using isometric exercise and aerobic 
training. Depending on the level of loss, the upper limb amputee 
should begin to practice several motions that will be needed to 
control the prosthesis. Additionally, the stability of the prosthesis 
depends on both the bulk of the stabilizing musculature and the 
amputee’s ability to voluntarily vary residual limb configuration.
Regardless of the rehabilitation pathway, early therapy should 
emphasize preserving range, strength, and maximizing use of the 
residual limb. Similar to the learned nonuse found in poststroke 
patients, upper extremity amputees may develop an early ten -
dency to almost exclusively use their remaining sound arm. T﻿hese 
habits can lead to less prosthetic success and a significant reduc -
tion in general functional potential.
Upper Limb Prostheses
An upper limb prosthesis is any device that replaces a portion of 
any upper limb that is missing; it can range from an artificial half 
finger worn solely to improve cosmesis to an electrically powered, 
computer controlled robotic arm meant to closely emulate an 
entire limb (Box 10.3).
Each prosthesis is unique and customized to the individual. 
Although two prostheses might be alike or very similar, there is no 
such thing as a normal or standard prosthesis. T﻿here are four 
broad categories of upper limb prosthetic systems: passive, body 
powered, myoelectric, and hybrid, which combines two or more 
of the others. T﻿he abilities of each system, and the requirements 
to use them, can vary significantly. T﻿he functional and vocational 
goals, the anticipated environmental exposures (such as sand, 
heat, or water), access to a prosthetist to build and maintain the 
device, and the financial resources of a patient all factor into pros-
thetic selection.
Passive or Cosmetic Prostheses
Some prosthetics are designed specifically to replicate the appear-
ance of the lost limb and provide unobtrusive cosmesis without 
powered movement. T﻿hese “passive” devices may still have joints 
or flexibility and are used functionally. T﻿hey may act as a stabiliz-
ing weight or be used as a point of contact to move objects or 
pinch objects between the passive device and the sound limb. 
T﻿hey are generally custom matched to the individual wearing 
them, including artificial nails, scars, and other skin changes to 
look more lifelike.
Body-Powered Prostheses
A body-powered system prosthesis uses the patient’s own residual 
limb or body strength and joint movement to control the prosthe-
sis (Fig. 10.4). Control includes powering the basic functions of 
terminal device opening and closing, elbow movement, and 
shoulder joint mobilization. T﻿he device may use the strength of 
the operating joint or body part to control the prosthetic joint, or 
it may use the body movement as a signal to externally power a 
joint. Most externally powered joints are battery operated to ac -
commodate the freedom and mobility desired by patients.
Myoelectric Prostheses
Myoelectric systems use the electrical activity generated by the 
voluntary contraction of a skeletal muscle to trigger the exter -
nally powered movement of a prosthetic joint ( Fig. 10.5). Myo-
electric controls use sensitive sensors that are carefully located 
superficial to key muscles, commonly the wrist extensor and 
flexor compartments of the forearm, or the bicep and tricep 
muscles of the upper arm. Well-controlled and properly timed 
contractions of these muscles can be challenging and require 
significant amounts of therapy and practice to use successfully. 
Different types of myoelectric control systems exist. T﻿he two-
site/two-function (dual-site) system has separate electrodes for 
paired prosthetic activity, such as flexion/extension or pronation/
Prosthetic Terminology
• Residual limb: The remaining portion of the amputated limb
• Excursion: Amount of movement or range of motion that the residual limb 
can achieve
• Component: A part of the prosthesis
• Terminal device: The prosthetic equivalent of the human hand
• Hinge: A prosthetic component used to assist or replace an anatomic joint
• Rigid hinge: Stiff/solid arrow movement in only one plane, usually flexion 
and extension
• Flexible hinge: Allows movement in multiple planes (i.e., flexion, extension, 
pronation, and supination)
• Socket: This part of the prosthesis acts as the interface between the resid -
ual limb and the prosthetic device as a whole. The socket is designed to 
distribute forces throughout the residual limb.
• Prosthetic sock: A prosthetic component, usually cotton, that fits the resid-
ual limb like a sock and is worn between the socket and liner to account 
for volume changes (size changes) in the residual limb
• Laminate: A plastic composite usually made with carbon fiber and resin
• Gel liner: A prosthetic component made from a silicone gel or similar poly -
mer that rolls onto the residual limb like a sleeve and creates a suction 
 interface between the skin and the socket
• Mechanical: Designates a moving component
• Passive: Describes a prosthesis used only for cosmesis rather than function
• Heavy duty: Connotes a prosthetic designed to withstand strong, repetitive 
forces and rugged conditions (e.g., a heavy-duty prosthetic user could be 
one who works as an automotive mechanic or construction worker)

Hybrid Prostheses
A hybrid system uses both the patient’s own muscle strength and 
joint movement as well as an external supply for power. An ex -
ample of a hybrid system is a body-powered elbow joint with an 
externally powered terminal device (Fig. 10.6).
Components: Socket and Suspension
T﻿here are multiple varieties of prosthetic sockets ( Fig. 10.7). T﻿he 
most appropriate choice depends on the characteristics of the re -
sidual limb, the type and purpose of the planned prosthetic com-
ponents, and the goals of the user. Self-suspended sockets benefit 
from strategically placed pressure points and bend to support the 
prosthesis wholly from the residual limb. Self-supported sockets 
tend to fit tightly and can require the use of donning aids, such as 
lubricants, to fully insert the residual limb. Harnesses can be used 
in place or in parallel with self-suspended styles of sockets to 
offload weight and gain support and stability from the remainder 
of the upper body. T wo popular styles are a figure-of-eight 
(Fig. 10.8) and a figure-of-nine that loop around both shoulders 
or only the contralateral shoulder, respectively. Body-powered 
prostheses may have thicker and/or additional straps to serve as 
attachments for the cables that operate the prosthetic joints. Care 
must be taken with harness design because the pressure from the 
straps can damage skin and soft tissues and compress nerves and 
blood vessels.
T﻿he trim lines of the upper extremity socket are dependent on 
the length of the residual limb. T﻿he shorter the limb, the higher the 
trim line. For a longer limb, the trim line is lower and allows more 
pronation-supination, especially in transradial users. T﻿he patient’s 
range of motion will be limited by a transradial prosthesis to ap-
proximately 70% of the motion possible without a prosthesis.
When the elbow joint is absent, the length of the transhumeral 
residual limb is a key factor in fitting and successful use of the 
prosthesis. Prosthetic control varies directly with the length of the 
humerus. Amputation through the distal third of the humerus 
provides functional control very similar to an elbow disarticula -
tion. T ransection of the humerus at least 4 inches above the olec-
ranon allows enough clearance to use all elbow options, including 
externally powered.
Forequarter-level amputations (loss of scapula, clavicle, and 
chest wall muscles) present even more of a challenge to fit with a 
functional prosthesis. Most control options have been removed 
with the residual limb. T﻿he “nudge” control, which is a force-sen-
sitive resistor operated by the chin, can operate the elbow and 
hand. If no prosthetic is used, a cosmetically sculpted insert should 
be offered for the shoulder symmetry needed for clothing fit.
Components: Shoulder
Shoulder joints are rarely used in prostheses due to the additional 
degrees of freedom and the greater control input required for 
meaningful use. Body-powered shoulder joints are more com -
mon, although cutting-edge prostheses may utilize an externally 
powered shoulder joint, often operated by foot pedals or other 
switches.
Components: Elbow
Elbow joints may be body or externally powered, with the choice 
often driven by the preference for terminal device control and the

Cable-operated terminal devices (hooks or hands) can be of a 
voluntary opening design (most common) or a voluntary closing 
design. With a voluntary opening mechanism, the terminal de -
vice is closed at rest. T﻿he patient uses the control-cable motion to 
open the terminal device against the resistive force of rubber 
bands (hook) or internal springs or cables (hand). Relaxation of 
the proximal muscles allows the terminal device to close around

Hooks have many advantages: T﻿hey are simple in design, 
lightweight, and durable; enable efficient grasp; have low main -

mechanical hand.
A prosthetic hand usually is bulkier and heavier than a hook. 
It can be powered by a cable or use external power. Some of the 
current hands on the market have unique grasping characteristics. 
T﻿hese include a feature used to eliminate slipping of an item being 
grasped. Most hands are capable of several grips; however, switch-

major grip types can be found in Box 10.4. Not all electronic

with the abovementioned systems or deviate enough to earn its 
own category.
Five Major Types of Grip
• Precision grip (i.e., pincher grip): The pad of the thumb and index finger are 
in opposition to pick up or pinch a small object (e.g., a small bead, pencil, 
grain of rice).
• Tripod grip (i.e., palmar grip, three-jaw chuck pinch): The pad of the thumb 
is against the pads of the index and middle finger.
• Lateral grip (i.e., key pinch): Tips of the fingers and thumb are flexed (e.g., 
when screwing in a light bulb or turning a doorknob).
• Hook power grip: The distal interphalangeal joint and proximal interphalan -
geal joint are flexed with the thumb extended (as when carrying a briefcase 
by the handle).
• Spherical grip: Tips of the fingers and thumb are flexed (e.g., when screw -
ing in a light bulb or opening a doorknob).

The Early Prosthetic Phase
Prosthetic Evaluation and Goals
When a patient with limb loss is being considered for a prosthesis, 
there are three initial areas of focus for the physiatrist.
First, has the residual limb fully healed from the surgery? Are 
there other new wounds or lesions since amputation? As in the 
lower extremity, adding a prosthesis adds pressure, heat, and weight 
to the underlying skin, thereby increasing the risk for damage and 
worsening existing skin issues. Has the patient already been pre -
scribed a residual limb shrinker to help decrease tissue edema and 
shape the limb in preparation for a prosthesis? If not, the patient 
should be able to tolerate the shrinker for 23 of 24 hours a day, 
removing for personal hygiene and cleansing of the shrinker.
Second, is pain present, either residual limb or phantom limb 
pain, that will interfere with potential prosthetic use? Pain can be 
treated concurrently during the prosthetic fitting and fabrication 
stages, but if wearing the final prosthesis causes pain, then there is 
a high potential for device abandonment.
T﻿hird, and perhaps most importantly, what are the goals of the 
potential prosthetic user? Understanding a patient’s current capa-
bilities, goals, and intended use of the artificial limb is paramount 
to providing a prosthesis that is desirable and useful. No artificial 
limb will be a true replacement for what was lost. Prosthetic limbs 
are tools used to help accomplish specific goals and tasks. Espe -
cially with upper extremity loss, having a frank conversation with 
the patient helps to set expectations and ultimately influence the 
acceptance and use of any provided limb.
Once a patient’s residual limb is ready for a prosthesis, pain is 
moderately to well controlled, and goals and expectations have 
been set in clinic, then a prosthetic prescription may be written. 
Like a medication prescription, there are certain required ele -
ments for the order. A specific diagnosis must be made (e.g., 
“Acquired absence of left upper limb above elbow Z89.222”), and 
the requested categories of components should be listed to in -
clude suspension type, socket type if needed, body powered or 
externally powered, and with or without myoelectric control if 
appropriate. Unlike lower extremities, upper limb prosthetics are 
not indicated based on a potential ambulation assessment or K 
level. T﻿he specific components and level of technology are nor -
mally determined based on the goals of the user and, ultimately, 
the insurance coverage and financial resources of the user. T﻿he 
cost of an upper extremity prosthesis can start at $3000 for a pas-
sive device, $10,000 for a body-powered hook, and $20,000 to 
$40,000 or more for an externally powered myoelectric arm. In-
surance, if present, may cover all the cost, part of the cost, or in 
some cases decline any reimbursement at all. While a patient’s 
health, capabilities, and goals should drive the primary decision 
making, it is important to understand and offer guidance on the 
financial realities of prosthetic costs. For veterans in the United 
States, note that the Veterans Affairs (VA) Hospital System oper-
ates a robust nationwide amputee rehabilitation network, and any 
veterans with limb loss should be encouraged to reach out to their 
local VA medical center to explore potential resources.
In most situations, it is beyond the physician’s knowledge and 
experience to fully prescribe the exact componentry of the pros -
thesis. Maintaining a clear line of communication with the pa -
tient’s prosthetist, or even better having them present in clinic, 
provides optimal quality and efficient care to the patient. Once 
prescribed, the patient will work with the prosthetist to develop a 
plan for the componentry, suspension system, and prosthetic 
socket. Often a clear plastic “check” socket will be produced to 
optimize the fit and function of the prosthesis. T﻿his allows moni-
toring of the skin and soft tissues of the residual limb during wear. 
Once the fit is finalized, a definitive socket, typically made of a 
plastic composite with carbon fiber and resin, will be produced 
and mounted to the prosthesis. After initial provision of the pros-
thesis, it is important for patients to follow up with their prosthe-
tist regularly to ensure optimal fit and function.
Ideally, a patient should follow up with the physician within 1 
to 2 months after receiving the definitive prosthesis so that a 
“checkout” visit may occur. T﻿his allows the patient to report any 
issues, such as pain or skin breakdown, and confirm that the de -
vice is meeting current needs. It also allows unforeseen problems 
with the prosthesis to be examined, and new prosthetic orders 
written if needed.
Early Prosthetic Training
T raining for the upper limb amputee should ideally begin before 
the surgery and continue until advanced training is completed. 
T﻿he training is divided into three phases: preprosthetic training, 
early prosthetic training, and advanced prosthetic training. Each 
phase is focused on the end goal of maximal functional adaptation 
and proficiency with the prosthesis. T﻿h e expectations of the

T﻿he prosthetic training phase begins with the delivery of the 
prosthesis. A key initial focus is on donning and doffing and wear-
ing the prosthesis for short periods with gradually increasing du -
ration of prosthetic wear. Integrating the prosthesis into daily ac -
tivity is the chief goal. Numerous issues arise during this period of 
rapid change, including maladaptive habits that occur quickly, 
such as inefficient body movements for control. After the intro -
duction to the prosthesis is completed, training progresses toward 
mastering basic ADLs (Box 10.5).
After ADLs are completed, the amputee is then moved to 
higher level homemaking skills and community reentry activities, 
such as driving, work, and recreation. Protocols for controls train-
ing for the body-powered prosthesis and the myoelectric prosthe-
sis have been reported by Ganz et al.15 Additionally, Northwestern 
University has designed a group of activities and a rating guide1 as 
a helpful tool for the therapist to use to guide and assess progress 
for the unilateral upper limb amputee ( Table 10.1).
Proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation enables the therapist 
to key into specific areas of muscle weakness. Isotonic exercises are 
effective in maintaining muscle bulk for stabilization of the arm 
in the socket of the prosthesis. T﻿he stability of the prosthesis de-
pends on both the bulk of the stabilizing musculature and the 
amputee’s ability to voluntarily vary residual limb configuration. 
For the transhumeral level, this would be the external rotators and 
the biceps that stabilize the socket; for the transradial level, it 
would be the supinators and pronators that stabilize the socket. 
T﻿here is a natural linear flow of these three rehabilitation phases. 
As the amputee attempts to integrate back into life, prosthetic 
training advances to mastering more specific and unique tasks 
(such as those found in Table 10.1). T﻿he prosthetic training phase 
ends with the proficient use of the prosthesis. Ultimately, success-
ful implementation of advanced prostheses will be largely contin-
gent on the availability of highly specialized and trained clinical 
personnel to fit and train amputees and resources to pay for these 
services.37
During prosthetic prescription and therapy, clinicians should 
consider using objective functional outcome measures to monitor 
a patient’s progress. Studied tools, such as the Patient Reported 
Outcome Measurement Information System (PROMIS) Upper 
Extremity Physical Function item bank, are relatively brief and 
were developed specifically to evaluate this population by the 
prosthetics company Hanger Clinic.13,20 T﻿he PROMIS tool is also 
available in a pediatric version.
Special Prosthetic Considerations
Bilateral Limb Loss
Bilateral amputees must use prostheses for all upper extremity 
activities. Artificial limbs can be much more challenging to don 
and doff, and care must be used in their design to help enable 
independence. Especially in heavy users, body-powered or hybrid 
systems with hooks tend to be favored by bilateral patients due to 
their durability, reliability, greater level of sensory feedback, and 
greater ease of donning/doffing. Additionally, prosthetic controls 
training is a more complex and coordinated motor process. T﻿he 
rehabilitation team needs to maintain and progress the strength 
and coordination gained in the pre-prosthetic training.
Burns and Skin Grafts
Amputations associated with trauma may also have damage to 
nearby soft tissues and skin or have skin grafts present after such 
damage. Skin grafts may take months to a year to fully mature, 
and it is important to communicate with the surgery team for 
discussion on prosthetic options and timeline.
Vision and/or Hearing Loss
Virtually all available prosthetic limbs lack sensory feedback and 
rely almost entirely on the user’s sight and hearing. When deficits

for irritation
• Controls training: Includes learning to control the individual components for 
operation. The five motion elements that are primarily used in hand manip -
ulation are reach, grasp, move, position, and release. The therapist uses 
many objects, such as wooded blocks, cotton swabs, and a sponge, in 
training the individual.

Traumatic Brain Injury and/or Cognitive Deficits
In patients with the inability to understand or follow safety in -
structions, a prosthetic device may provide more risk than benefit. 
If a prosthetic is to be prescribed, its reliability and ease of use 
should be prioritized. Discussion and shared decision making 
with the patient and the family is critical to determining goals and 
prosthetic choices.
The Advanced Prosthetic Phase
Once patients’ residual limbs have stabilized in size (which can 
take $1 year), swelling has reduced, wounds have fully healed, 
and they have begun to successfully use a prosthetic device, then 
they transition into the advanced phase of rehabilitation. T﻿he 
goals of advanced prosthetic rehabilitation focus on maintaining 
and optimizing function, minimizing pain, preventing common 
complications, and adapting to the patient’s medical and lifestyle 
changes.
As a general guideline, patients with upper extremity amputa -
tion who have reached the advanced prosthetic phase should be 
followed up at least yearly. At these visits, the clinician should 
assess the basic domains found in Box 10.6. Some of these needs 
that are specific to the upper extremity will be discussed in this 
chapter, and others will be covered in detail in the chapter on 
lower extremity limb loss.
While the need for regular lifelong follow-up by the rehabilita-
tion team cannot be overemphasized, it is frequently underuti -
lized. Without follow-up, many barriers to successful prosthetic 
use cannot be addressed, and functional use can be greatly dimin-
ished or absent. Issues such as pain, depression, skin irritation, 
limb size change, and changes in activity levels or use are all more 
easily addressed early. In some cases, serious complications, such 
as limb infection, falls, or prosthetic failure, can be averted 
through preventative care at these visits. Rehabilitation physicians 
should work proactively to prevent complications when possible. 
T﻿hey play a key role in the health and safety of an amputee. In the 
case of an otherwise healthy, traumatic amputee, the rehabilita-
tion physician may be the only regular point of contact with the 
health care system.
Patients should first be assessed for any significant medical or 
life changes since the last evaluation. Physicians should inquire

Prosthetic fit is an important aspect of amputee care that fre -
quently changes over a patient’s lifetime. Volume change in the 
residual limb is common, especially within the first year after 
amputation. Upper extremities are less susceptible to volume 
changes than the lower extremities due to gravity, but nonetheless 
may require adjustments of the prosthetic socket or new choices 
of suspension. If a patient is experiencing significant changes 
throughout a given day, new suspension strategies may be indi -
cated, along with wearing compression garments. Significant 
weight changes can severely impair prosthetic fit, especially in the 
upper extremity where sockets are fit very intimately to the pa -
tient’s anatomy. It is critical to ascertain the medical reason for the 
patient’s apparent weight gain because serious conditions such 
congestive heart failure, renal and hepatic disease, venous occlu -
sion, metabolic syndromes, or changes in medications such as 
diuretics should not be missed.
Pain Assessment and Management
Pain in the amputee population can be categorized as residual 
limb pain , originating from the intact portion of the limb, or 
phantom limb pain , originating from the amputated limb that is 
no longer present. Residual limb pain has a wide differential diag-
nosis and encompasses both amputation-related and general 
musculoskeletal and neurologic conditions. As discussed previ -
ously, neuromas formed after the amputation are common sources 
of both residual limb and phantom limb pain. T﻿heir clinical 
management is discussed in detail in the chapter on lower limb 
amputation. Surgical procedures mentioned previously in this 
chapter, such as TMR and RPNI, are emerging and promising 
management options. Musculoskeletal pathologies, such as arthri-
tis, tendinopathy, bursitis, or ligament tears, may present as re -
sidual limb pain. It is important to recall that patients with am -
putations may also develop complex regional pain syndrome, and 
this should be included on any pain differential, particularly when 
pain appears neuropathic in nature with vasomotor or skin find -
ings or when these symptoms are temporally delayed from the 
amputation event.12
Phantom limb pain is a unique problem to the amputee popu-
lation. Clinically, it is best understood as a maladaptive neuroplas-
tic response to the loss of the limb. In these cases, the brain inter-
prets the limb’s afferent nerve signals, or lack thereof, as pain. It 
has both central and peripheral mechanisms, which despite exten-
sive research remain poorly understood.42 It is typically described 
as a neuropathic sensation, with burning, stabbing sensations that 
wax and wane, and is often worse at nighttime. Patients may have 
difficulty in clearly describing their pain. T﻿he prevalence of phan-
tom limb pain in the general amputee population has been esti -
mated at 64% in a recent meta-analysis, although estimates vary 
widely depending on methodology and population studied. 28 In 
most of these patients, symptoms are intermittent and may arise 
occasionally for seconds to minutes before resolving, and it may 
not significantly impair their functionality or quality of life. In 
some patients, however, phantom limb pain can become a 
chronic, severe, and debilitating disease that is notoriously diffi -
cult to treat. T reatment strategies for phantom limb pain of the 
upper limb are like those discussed for the lower limb in the fol -
lowing chapter. T﻿hese include medical, therapeutic, and surgical 
management. Unfortunately, scientific evidence for best practice

limb still being present. T﻿he prevalence of phantom limb sensa-

Musculoskeletal Comorbidities
Patients with upper extremity amputations are at higher risk than 
the average population for other common musculoskeletal prob -
lems, such as neck pain or rotator cuff pathology. 44 T﻿he manage-
ment of most of these conditions is not unique to amputees and 
is discussed in other chapters. Patients should be screened for pain 
and dysfunction of all proximal joints and muscles to their ampu-
tation. Some patients may develop an amputation-specific bursi -
tis, tendinopathy, or enthesopathy in their residual limb. Surgical 
clips and other retained hardware contribute to risk of pain near 
the operative site. T﻿he sound limb should also be screened for 
pathology, with particular attention for overuse injuries such as 
carpal tunnel or rotator cuff pathology. T﻿he optimal health of the 
sound limb is of paramount importance and should not be ne -
glected during rehabilitation or follow-up appointments.
Heterotopic Ossification
Heterotopic ossification (HO) is the pathologic formation of 
bone outside of existing skeletal tissue. It can present with signifi-
cant pain and forms within weeks or months after amputation. In 
upper extremity amputees, symptomatic HO can cause pain, skin 
breakdown, and ultimately may impair the use of a prosthesis. It 
is often palpable in the residual limb or detectable by x-ray or 
ultrasound. T riple-phase bone scan is not usually required unless 
x-ray imaging appears negative with high clinical suspicion. T﻿he 
mainstays of conservative treatment are activity modification, 
prosthesis adjustment to accommodate limb changes, and physi -
cal therapy to maintain range of motion. 31 T﻿here is no available 
strong evidence for any medical intervention to slow the progres -
sion of HO in the amputee population. Nonsteroidal antiinflam-
matory drugs, bisphosphonates, and local radiation have been 
trialed as prophylactic and therapeutic options in other patient 
populations but have not been proven in the amputee population 
at this time.33 Other medications that have been trialed but have 
limited evidence include colchicine, corticosteroids, and calcito -
nin.29,32 HO can be surgically excised. Classically, this is per -
formed once it has fully matured, which takes roughly 6 to 
18  months. 19 However, it is now becoming more common to 
proceed with earlier excision as long as the patient has failed con-
servative measures, and the HO is corticated and well defined on 
imaging.34 In extensive or nonresectable cases, amputation revi -
sion may be considered instead.
Dermatologic Management
Skin condition must also be assessed at each visit. Residual limb 
skin care and the management of common amputee skin issues 
are discussed in detail in the chapter on lower extremity limb loss. 
Skin irritation leading to blistering, skin breakdown, and topical 
infection are common, especially with poor prosthetic fit. T﻿his is 
most commonly due to a worn prosthetic suspension, such as a 
damaged gel liner, excessive use, or abrupt changes in wear time. 
Other common etiologies include skin irritation due to skin 
creams, antiperspirants, and other topical lotions or medications. 
Patients should be encouraged to clean and moisturize their re -
sidual limb daily, but lotions should never be applied just prior to 
donning a liner or prosthesis. For soft tissue infections, topical 
and antibiotic treatment may be indicated, and prosthetic wear 
should be limited to encourage healing. Common skin conditions 
seen in amputees are discussed in greater detail in the chapter on 
lower extremity amputees, but include acroangiodermatitis, ver -
rucous hyperplasia, and epidermoid cysts. Acroangiodermatitis is 
a condition that produces painful or pruritic purplish papules and 
plaques due to venous insufficiency and stimuli from socket wear. 
Verrucous hyperplasia is a condition resulting in wartlike over -
growth on the residual limb, usually caused by venous pooling in 
the distal end due to poor prosthetic fit.35 Epidermoid cysts of the 
residual limb may also develop and can be treated with excision if 
symptomatic.26 Skin infection of the residual limb is not uncom -
mon. It is frequently caused by contact dermatitis blistering, pres-
sure ulceration, or bacterial or fungal cellulitis. Prosthetic rest and 
hygiene can minimize this risk. Deeper infection, including septic 
bursitis, is also possible and typically presents with a swollen, red, 
and painful limb. Severe cases may require hospital admission to 
avoid limb compromise and sepsis.
Hyperhidrosis
Excessive sweating (hyperhidrosis) of the residual limb can impair 
prosthetic use and be distressing to the patient, particularly in hot 
climates or with high-activity demands. Prosthetic sockets can 
sometimes accommodate drainage or breathability, or additional 
socket layers may be used to absorb perspiration. T opical antiper-
spirant (preferably without deodorant to avoid skin irritation) is 
often an effective solution and is recommended to be applied at 
bedtime rather than in the morning to avoid skin irritation after 
donning a prosthetic. In refractive cases, botulinum toxin may be 
injected into the skin at areas of excessive sweating and, if effec -
tive, may last up to several months or more before needing to be 
repeated.
Rehabilitation Goals and Prosthetic Prescription
At each clinic visit, the patient and care team should reassess their 
progress and goals and determine if any change in course is 
needed. Are patients and the team satisfied with their progress? 
Have patients changed jobs or started a new hobby? Have they 
achieved their goals and potential? Are there barriers preventing 
this? Is there new technology or treatment that might benefit 
them? Some of these might be best addressed with occupational 
or vocational therapy or assistive/adaptive devices. Prosthetic 
equipment such as socks, liners, or components may be worn and 
need replacement. T﻿he frequency of replacement can change 
based on use and environment, but new liners and socks every  
6 months is reasonable; it may be more frequent if the items are 
found to be worn. Changes in the patient’s body or activities may 
necessitate adjustment of an existing prosthetic or the prescription 
of a new one to meet their needs. Note that activity-specific pros-
thetics may not be covered by most insurance plans depending on 
the indication. Many activity-specific subtypes of prosthetics, 
such as recreational limbs, water-resistant prostheses (“water 
legs”), and those made for cosmetic purposes are unfortunately 
not covered by most insurance due to being deemed not medi -
cally necessary. T﻿here is no firm rule on replacement of an upper

Upper Limb Loss Adaptation and 
Reintegration
While a well-fitting prosthesis can provide significant utility, it is 
not a replacement for the lost limb. Even if ADLs are easily ac -
complished by the patient, it is likely that there are multiple as -
pects of their home, vocation, or hobbies that will require adapta-
tion and modification. A consultation to a physical therapist or 
occupational therapist for a home safety evaluation, use of adap -
tive equipment, and home modifications must be considered. As 
in lower extremity amputation, the self-perception of a patient 
with limb loss can be severely altered or impaired, and early and 
regular visits with mental health providers can assist with adjust -
ment and body image concerns. T﻿hese psychological changes can 
also significantly affect relationships with spouses, families, and 
friends, and it may prove useful for those with partners to par -
ticipate in couples counseling. In almost all cases of upper extrem-
ity amputation, the ability of the patient to work will be impaired. 
Consideration should be made for consultations for vocational 
rehabilitation, requests for accommodation at work, and ensuring 
a relationship with a social worker or case manager to assist with 
disability applications as appropriate.
Psychological Reintegration, Community 
Support, and Disability
Limb loss is a profound life change, and regular attention should 
be paid to patients’ mood and feelings about their limb loss. 
 Depression risk is elevated during the first 2 years after amputation 
in multiple studies,21,24 but little data exist for long-term follow-
up. Psychological counseling or referral to psychiatry can also be 
helpful and should be considered part of regular follow-up.
T﻿he limb loss community is particularly active worldwide 
through support groups, advocacy, and media. Connecting pa -
tients to these resources can play an important supplementary role 
in their recovery. Local amputation support groups meet regularly 
in many midsize and large cities to connect patients with peers. 
T﻿he ACA is a very active national group that provides peer sup -
port, education, and advocacy for those with limb loss. T﻿he orga-
nization publishes educational items such as books and pamphlets 
(often in multiple languages and sizes of text) and holds large 
annual conventions for those with limb loss, their family and 
friends, and health care professionals. Other resources can be 
found through organizations such as the Veterans Affairs Health 
System or through sponsored events organized by nonprofits or 
prosthetic companies. T﻿here are also amputee-centric publica -
tions, such as Amplitude Magazine, that provide an educational 
and media outlet focused on this community. T﻿he educational 
materials available to patients through these groups span a wide 
variety of topics, from insurance navigation, legal advocacy, 
 research developments, and resources to less discussed issues, 
 including sex and intimacy. Interacting with these groups can 
provide an important sense of belonging and support during a 
period of intense psychological adjustment.
Many aspects of upper limb prosthetic rehabilitation cannot be 
addressed until the patient has had reasonable time to become 
acclimated to the life and functional changes being experienced. 
T﻿hese include housing and employment concerns. At each follow-
up, patients should be evaluated for potential social services refer-
rals. T﻿he physiatrist may also be asked by insurance to state the 
patient’s level of disability (Table 10.2).
T﻿he team should remain available to address questions and 
nurture newly mastered skills. T﻿he fit, comfort, and function of 
the prosthesis must be maintained and optimized over time as 
amputees alter and refine their initial goals and aspirations. T﻿he 
successful long-term use of an upper limb prosthesis depends 
primarily on its comfort and its perceived value to the amputee. 
Innovative design and careful custom adaptation of socket and 
harness, careful attention to follow-up adjustments, and prescrip-
tion revisions based on the amputee’s changing needs are the es -
sential factors for successful prosthetic rehabilitation.
Emerging Technology
T﻿he sheer amount of new and developing technology could easily 
fill a textbook of its own. What follows is a short overview of 
trends and new areas of innovation.
 1. Powered prosthetics. T﻿here have been motorized versions of all 
upper extremity prosthetic joints for many years. Most exter -
nally powered hands, however, tend to have motors in the 
thumb and first three digits only. Newer models can have mo-
tors in all digits, allowing individual movement of the fingers 
as well as flexion and opposition of the thumb. Even more 
advanced devices allow finger abduction and adduction.
 2. T﻿hree-dimensional (3D) printing and digital design. Other -
wise known as additive manufacturing, 3D printing allows an 
object to be built layer by layer from a material, typically a 
plastic polymer, from a digital design. In the future, 3D print-
ing may allow patients who do not have access to a prosthetist 
to access custom-fit artificial limbs.
 3. Pattern recognition. T raditional myoelectric limbs use one or 
two small sensors directly over muscles to capture muscle 
movement. Newer input devices can have eight or more sites 
spread circumferentially around a limb and use computers to 
recognize patterns as patients emulate certain movements. 
T﻿hese patterns can be used as triggers for motorized joints, 
 allowing more precise and intuitive control.
 4. Targeted muscle reinnervation and regenerative peripheral 
nerve interfaces. T﻿hese have been previously covered in the 
surgical section of this chapter; however, it is important to re -
iterate that a major barrier in more capable prosthetic limbs is 
the ability to control them. More advanced functions generally

Pediatric Amputation and Congenital  
Limb Difference
T﻿here are special considerations for amputation care in the pe -
diatric patient with upper extremity amputation and congenital 
limb loss or limb difference. About 4 of every 10,000 babies 
each year are born with at least partial limb loss. In approxi -
mately 58% of cases, this is due to congenital deficiencies in 
which the limb bud fails to fully form in utero, but usually it 
does not have genetic or hereditary implications for the patient 
and the family. T eratogens and amniotic band syndrome are 
two of the most common causes. T﻿he International Society for 
Prosthetics and Orthotics terminology for congenital limb re -
ductions is described in Box 10.7, which divides limb malfor -
mations into transverse or longitudinal. By definition, a child 
who has a transverse deficiency has no distal remaining parts 
beyond the named bone. For example, a child with a transverse 
radial deficiency has a normal upper arm and a portion of the 
radius but is missing the hand and fingers. Longitudinal defi -
ciencies have distal portions present with a partial or total ab -
sence of a specific bone. T﻿his naming convention is sometimes 
used alongside the older Greek-based nomenclature shown in 
Box 10.8
, which is still used in some environments. T﻿he most 
common congenital limb deficiency in the upper limb is a lon-
gitudinal partial or complete lack of the radius. Longitudinal 
hand reductions represent half of all congenital upper limb re -
ductions, and multiple limb reductions are found in less than 
20% of live births. Acquired amputations in children have a 
wide variety of causes but are most commonly due to trauma.
3 
Medical causes such as cancer, frostbite, vascular occlusion, or 
tumors also contribute.

their developmental stage. Young children may receive their first

midline to manipulate objects, which is generally around 6 months 
of age. T﻿his first prosthetic is usually a passive upper extremity 
device. Children may progress to more complex prosthetic compo-

life, then every 18 months between ages 5 and 12, and then bian-

used so that each layer can be removed to accommodate growth. 
Length should also be adjusted to match patients’ body propor -

chosen during surgery to preserve skeletal growth potential, such 
as joint disarticulations. In cases where this is not possible, multi-
ple surgeries may be needed over the course of their development. 
T﻿here are also children with congenital limb deficiencies who are

In skeletally immature children with an amputation of any

bony overgrowth of the residual limb. T﻿his occurs in 40% of

examined by x-ray, ultrasound, or other imaging. Surgical revision

remain high even after surgery.39 After amputation, limb shaping 
and edema control are treated similarly to adults. Shrinker socks 
are not generally available in pediatric sizes, so limb edema is usu-

casting their prosthetic.27 Children who use a prosthetic should be 
followed up at least once a year (or even more frequently during 
a growth spurt) to monitor their progress and growth and to 
troubleshoot issues with the device, supplies, equipment, skin, 
musculoskeletal complications, or functional deficits. For chil -

Summary
Upper limb amputation is a life-altering event that profoundly 
impacts physical function, emotional well-being, and social iden-
tity. While the condition carries significant psychological and 
functional challenges, advances in surgical technique, prosthetic 
technology, and interdisciplinary rehabilitation teams have greatly 
improved the outlook for affected individuals. T﻿here is a wide 
spectrum of upper limb amputations and therefore functional 
implications. Pre- and post-surgical planning plays a critical role 
in optimizing outcomes, including decisions about limb length, 
tissue management techniques, and pain management strategies 
such as Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR) and Regenerative 
Peripheral Nerve Interface (RPNI). T﻿he rehabilitation process, 
ideally led by an experienced physiatrist, includes early interven -
tions to manage pain, promote healing, and maintain joint mobil-
ity, followed by prosthetic evaluation, fitting, and then training 
phases tailored to individual goals and functional needs. T﻿here are 
many prosthetic types—passive, body-powered, myoelectric, and 
hybrid—and components, allowing patient-centric customiza -
tion. It is important to be aware of potentially complex consider -
ations such as bilateral amputations, pediatric limb differences, 
cognitive impairment, and comorbidities like skin issues, hetero -
topic ossification, and phantom limb pain, as these can easily and 
significantly change functional outcomes. Long-term follow-up is 
essential for monitoring prosthetic fit, addressing complications, 
and supporting psychological reintegration and vocational needs. 
Emerging technologies such as osseointegration, brain-computer 
interfaces, and 3D-printed limbs offer promising future advance -
ments. Ultimately, successful rehabilitation hinges on an experi -
enced interdisciplinary team, patient-centric goals, and ongoing 
support.
